Post_Text: Before you ask "are we covered," ask a better question. What is your school actually trying to protect against? Someone taking your funds and your network getting hit are two different disasters. One policy does not answer both:
This school was proud of its cyber policy. Then an employee walked off with the money, and the carrier said no. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)

A longtime employee in the business office moved money out for months. The school filed on its cyber policy. Denied. Nobody hacked the network, so cyber never saw a loss. Employee theft is a crime policy loss, and they didn't have one:

Crime and cyber answer two different disasters. The real question is what your school is trying to protect against, because you need both plus the seam between them. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)
Notes: Post from company page; consider PDF lead-capture version

Post_Text: Now run it the other way. Ransomware locks your student information system the week before enrollment. You file on your crime policy. Denied. Nobody stole your money in the sense crime means. That is why a school needs both, not the better-sounding one:

A criminal poses as a known vendor and emails new banking details. Your business manager updates the file and sends the payment. The cyber carrier says no breach. The crime carrier says no break-in. That seam needs its own endorsement, or both policies point at each other:

You can carry crime and cyber and still lose the wire-fraud claim. The endorsement that covers it usually rides a sublimit far below your main limit. Cap it at $25,000 against a $200,000 payment and you have a partial answer dressed up as a full one. Here is how to size it to your actual wires:

The cheapest fraud control your school has costs nothing. Any change to vendor banking details gets confirmed by phone to a known number, never the number in the email. Some carriers won't even pay without it. It stops most of these losses cold:

Three questions for whoever handles your school's insurance: If an employee stole from your accounts, which policy pays? If ransomware locked your systems, which policy pays? If a fake vendor email got a payment sent, which policy pays, and what's the limit? If any answer is "I think we have something for that," read this:
